note: Health Check exposes a single fixed route with no config, no plugins, and no permissions. Cases are `recipe` (response-graded) since they probe documented facts an agent would otherwise read source to answer; no site mutation.

evals.prompt: On a Drupal site with the health_check module enabled, what URL path do I give a load balancer to check the instance's health, and what is the route name?
evals.expected_output: The path is /health (route name health_check.content). The path is fixed and not configurable.

evals.prompt: What HTTP status code and response body does the health_check module's endpoint return so a load balancer or uptime monitor can tell the instance is healthy?
evals.expected_output: It returns HTTP 200 with a UNIX timestamp (time()) as a text/plain body; a non-200 means the instance is unhealthy.

evals.prompt: Which controller and method handle the health_check endpoint, and what does the method return?
evals.expected_output: HealthController::content() returns a plain Symfony Response with body time(), status 200, and Content-Type text/plain.

evals.prompt: Why does polling the health_check /health endpoint always hit the live instance instead of returning a cached copy? Which route option controls this?
evals.expected_output: The route sets no_cache: TRUE, so the response is never cached and every request re-hits the live instance; the returned timestamp changes each time.

evals.prompt: Does the health_check /health endpoint still respond while the Drupal site is in maintenance mode, and which route option makes that possible?
evals.expected_output: Yes; the route sets _maintenance_access: TRUE, so /health answers 200 even in maintenance mode.

evals.prompt: Can I change the /health path or add custom pluggable subsystem checks using the health_check module's own configuration?
evals.expected_output: No. The module has no settings form, no config, and no pluggable checks; the /health path is fixed and only returns the timestamp.

evals.prompt: What does a 200 from the health_check /health endpoint actually confirm about the Drupal instance, and what happens if the instance can't bootstrap?
evals.expected_output: A 200 with a fresh timestamp confirms PHP and Drupal request handling are alive without rendering a full page; if the instance cannot bootstrap it returns a 5xx (or fails) and the load balancer can drop it.
